meta.title: Context update causes rerender storm
meta.summary: One provider holds too much frequently changing state, so unrelated consumers rerender on every tiny update.
signals: Provider value identity changes every update

context.symptom: Typing in a filter field causes chart legends, notification badges, and sidebar widgets to rerender even though their visible data did not change.
context.userImpact: The page feels heavy, and low-end machines stutter during interactions that should be local.
context.environment: A single app-wide React context provides auth, theme, notifications, filters, drafts, and callbacks in one object.

evidence.title: Profiler fan-out
evidence.body: One provider update fans out to 47 consumers; most render outputs are identical.

evidence.title: Provider value
Observed pattern
evidence.body: Even consumers that only read theme rerender when draft text changes.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.helperText: Choose the explanation that matches broad rerender fan-out, not one specific child component.

options.label: A broad provider value is recreated on every change, so all consumers observing that context rerender together.
options.feedback: Correct. Context propagation and unstable provider identity are the core problem here.

options.label: The issue is simply that every consumer forgot to use React.memo.
options.feedback: Memo can help local props, but it does not stop context updates from reaching consumers.

options.label: The main bottleneck is browser layout thrash, not React updates.
options.feedback: Layout can matter, but the profiler already shows broad React rerender fan-out as the immediate issue.

options.label: React should deep-compare the provider value, so the real issue is a framework bug.
options.feedback: React context does not work that way. Designing narrower update boundaries is the application responsibility.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: Rank the three most useful debug steps first.
stages.helperText: You need to prove where the fan-out starts before choosing a state architecture.

candidates.label: Profile the interaction and identify which consumers rerender after a provider update.
Inspect the provider value shape and whether its identity changes on unrelated updates.
Verify that consumers reading stable slices still rerender because they subscribe to the broad context.
Wrap every consumer in React.memo before you confirm the source of the fan-out.
Change the CSS framework to reduce rerenders.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes belong in a real fix?
stages.helperText: Select the changes that reduce update scope, not just the ones that hide symptoms.

options.label: Split the broad provider into narrower contexts based on update frequency and responsibility.
options.feedback: Correct. Narrower subscription boundaries reduce unnecessary consumer updates.

options.label: Move frequently changing shared state to a selector-based store or equivalent subscription model.
options.feedback: Correct. Selector-based reads can prevent unrelated consumers from rerendering.

options.label: Stabilize provider values and callbacks where the architecture still uses context.
options.feedback: Correct. Stable identities matter, though they are not enough alone if the provider stays too broad.

options.label: Rely only on React.memo around consumers to stop context rerenders.
options.feedback: Memo alone does not solve broad context propagation.

options.label: Deep-compare the provider value on every render so React can skip updates.
options.feedback: Deep comparison is expensive and still misses the structural issue of overly broad subscription scope.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best regression guard after the fix?
stages.helperText: Pick something that protects rerender scope instead of relying on intuition.

options.label: Add a focused render-count or profiler-based regression around an unrelated consumer during draft updates.
options.feedback: Correct. This guards the exact fan-out problem that hurt the interaction.

options.label: Track API latency because server speed affects typing responsiveness.
options.feedback: That does not guard the local rerender scope issue.

options.label: Open the profiler once before major releases and trust the architecture.
options.feedback: Manual profiling helps, but it is too weak as the primary guardrail.

options.label: Stop adding shared state to the page so the context can never grow again.
options.feedback: That blocks product work instead of enforcing healthy state boundaries.

scoreBands.label: Fan-out not fully isolated
scoreBands.summary: You saw slow renders, but not the subscription-boundary problem inside the provider.

scoreBands.label: Good direction, incomplete boundary fix
scoreBands.summary: You identified context as the issue, but one step still leaned on symptom-level fixes.

scoreBands.label: State-boundary reasoning
scoreBands.summary: You diagnosed the broad provider, chose structural fixes, and guarded rerender scope directly.

idealRunbook: Profile the interaction and count affected consumers.
Inspect provider identity and value scope.
Reduce subscription breadth with narrower contexts or selectors.
Protect the fix with a rerender-scope regression.

teachingBlocks.title: Interview shortcut
teachingBlocks.text: When context performance comes up, talk about subscription boundaries first. Memoization is secondary.

teachingBlocks.title: Better answer framing
items: Name the broad provider and unstable identity.
Explain why React.memo alone is insufficient.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If you kept context, what would make you split it into two providers instead of moving to a store?
